era_context: The Warring States period in China is in its final phase, with Qin Shihuang's forces consolidating control over most of the known Chinese world. In the Mediterranean, the Second Punic War is in its early stages. The Hellenistic world is fragmented among the successors of Alexander. In India, the Mauryan Empire is declining.

events.title: Qin forces conquer Chu state
events.description: Qin forces, under the command of Meng Tian and other generals, conquer the Chu state, one of the largest and most powerful of the remaining independent states. The conquest of Chu is a major milestone in Qin Shihuang's unification campaign. Chu, located in the Yangtze River valley, had been a major power throughout the Warring States period. Its conquest brings vast territories and resources under Qin control and leaves only Qi and Yan as significant independent powers.

sources.name: Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian) by Sima Qian
Qin administrative records and archaeological evidence

events.certainty_note: The conquest of Chu is attested in the Shiji and corroborated by archaeological evidence. However, the precise dating of this conquest within the 223–221 BCE period is somewhat uncertain; some sources place it in 223 BCE, others in 222 BCE. The Shiji's chronology is generally reliable but may have minor dating uncertainties.

events.title: Seleucus II dies; Antiochus III becomes Seleucid king
events.description: Seleucus II Callinicus, king of the Seleucid Empire, dies after a reign marked by internal strife and loss of eastern territories. He is succeeded by his younger brother Antiochus III, who will become known as 'the Great.' Antiochus III inherits a weakened kingdom but will spend his reign attempting to restore Seleucid power through military campaigns. The transition of power is marked by court intrigue and the influence of the regent Hermias.
events.certainty_note: Seleucus II's death and Antiochus III's accession are well-documented in Polybius and later sources. The date 222 BCE is secure.

events.title: Ptolemy III Euergetes dies; Ptolemy IV Philopator accedes
events.description: Ptolemy III Euergetes, who ruled Egypt for 25 years, dies and is succeeded by his son Ptolemy IV Philopator. This succession marks a transition in Ptolemaic power dynamics. Ptolemy IV's reign is characterized by internal court factions and the influence of powerful advisors, particularly Sosibius. Despite these challenges, Ptolemy IV will achieve a notable military victory against Antiochus III at the Battle of Raphia in 217 BCE.
events.certainty_note: Ptolemaic succession dates are well-established through papyrological evidence. Ptolemy III's death and Ptolemy IV's accession in 221 or 222 BCE are secure; sources vary slightly on the exact year.

events.title: Second Punic War: Early campaigns in Spain and Italy
events.description: The Second Punic War (218–201 BCE) is in its early phase. Roman and Carthaginian forces are engaged in Spain and the western Mediterranean. Hannibal is consolidating Carthaginian power in Spain and preparing for his famous campaign across the Alps. Roman forces under various commanders are attempting to contain Carthaginian expansion. This period marks the beginning of a conflict that will last nearly two decades and fundamentally alter the balance of power in the Mediterranean.
events.certainty_note: The Second Punic War is well-documented in multiple classical sources. The year 222 BCE falls within the early phase of the conflict, which is well-attested.

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of the conquest of Chu is uncertain; some sources place it in 223 BCE rather than 222 BCE. The Shiji's chronology for the Warring States period is generally reliable but may have minor dating uncertainties of ±1–2 years. The succession dates for Seleucus II and Ptolemy III vary slightly among sources; some place these events in 221 BCE rather than 222 BCE. The precise sequence of events in the early Second Punic War is sometimes unclear in surviving sources.
historiographic_note: Chinese sources are dominated by the Shiji, compiled centuries after the events. Archaeological evidence from Qin-period administrative documents provides corroboration but is fragmentary. For the Mediterranean, Polybius and Livy are primary sources, both written well after the events. Egyptian sources are primarily administrative papyri. The Seleucid Empire is documented primarily through Greek and later sources. All ancient sources on the Second Punic War are written by Romans or later authors dependent on Roman sources, introducing potential bias toward Roman perspectives.

graph_edges.note: Conquest of Chu is a major step toward Qin unification completed in 221 BCE
Antiochus III's accession initiates a period of Seleucid expansion
Ptolemy IV's accession leads to his military confrontation with Antiochus III
Early Second Punic War campaigns lead to Hannibal's crossing of the Alps
